Types of Meditation: 9 Styles Explained

There are many meditation styles — mindfulness, loving-kindness, body scan, transcendental, breath, walking, chakra, third-eye, and visualization. This guide compares them so you can choose what fits you.

  • Crystals: Amethyst, Clear Quartz

What Is Types of Meditation?

There are many meditation styles — mindfulness, loving-kindness, body scan, transcendental, breath, walking, chakra, third-eye, and visualization. This guide compares them so you can choose what fits you.

Types of Meditation Explained

When exploring types of meditation, practitioners often encounter overlapping techniques that share roots across ancient Asian lineages and modern psychological research. This guide outlines nine distinct approaches—mindfulness, loving-kindness, body scan, transcendental, breath, walking, chakra, third-eye, and visualization—so you can match a practice to your daily rhythm. Each method invites a different focal point, whether that is counting inhalations, tracking physical sensations, or holding a silent phrase. Choosing among types of meditation depends less on finding a single correct path and more on identifying which anchor supports your current mental state. Note: consistency matters more than session length when building attention habits.

How to Practice

  1. Step 1: Mindfulness — present-moment awareness
  2. Step 2: Loving-Kindness (Metta) — cultivating compassion
  3. Step 3: Body Scan — systematic body awareness
  4. Step 4: Transcendental — mantra-based
  5. Step 5: Breath (Anapanasati) — breath focus
  6. Step 6: Walking — mindful movement
  7. Step 7: Chakra — energy center focus
  8. Step 8: Third-Eye — intuition focus
  9. Step 9: Visualization — guided imagery

Meaning & Benefits

Mindfulness anchors attention in the present moment. Sit comfortably, close your eyes, and notice the sensation of air at the nostrils for three full minutes. When thoughts arise, label them briefly as “planning” or “remembering,” then return focus to the immediate environment.

Loving-Kindness (Metta) cultivates compassion through structured phrases. Begin with yourself, silently repeating “May I be safe, may I be healthy, may I be at ease” for twenty breaths. Extend the sequence outward: first to a loved one, then a neutral person, and finally to someone you find challenging, maintaining the exact same phrasing.

Body Scan builds systematic awareness by moving attention through physical regions. Start at the left big toe, hold focus for five natural breaths, note any temperature or tension, then shift upward to the ankle, knee, thigh, hip, and continue sequentially to the crown of the head.

Transcendental practice relies on a silent, personal mantra. After settling into a comfortable seated posture, repeat your chosen sound quietly in your mind, allowing syllables to blend together without forcing rhythm. If the mind wanders, gently resume the phonetic pattern until the session concludes.

Breath (Anapanasati) focuses directly on inhalation and exhalation. In the Theravada tradition, practitioners track four phases: counting breaths one through ten, observing the full length of the inhale, noting the complete exhale, and resting awareness at the upper lip. Repeat this cycle for twelve minutes.

Walking translates stillness into motion. Step forward slowly, placing the heel down first, rolling through the arch, and lifting the toes. Sync each step with a count of three seconds. When pacing outdoors, align your gaze fifteen feet ahead rather than looking downward.

Chakra meditation centers on anatomical energy points. Visualize seven vertical spheres aligned along the spine. Beginning at the base, imagine each sphere glowing a distinct color for three breaths. Progress upward to the throat, chest, solar plexus, heart, brow, and crown, adjusting posture to keep the spine elongated.

Third-Eye work targets intuition through focused gaze. Sit facing a candle or dark wall, draw attention to the space between the eyebrows, and hold that point of concentration for ten minutes. Allow peripheral vision to soften while keeping internal attention steady on the midpoint.

Visualization employs guided imagery to structure mental space. Close your eyes and construct a detailed room: note the floor texture, the height of the walls, the direction of light entering through a window. Spend eight minutes moving through this imagined space, adding sensory details like scent or ambient temperature before ending the session.

Supporting CrystalsAmethyst, Clear Quartz

Crystals to Deepen Your Practice

Many meditators pair their practice with stones that offer tactile grounding and visual reference points. Rather than treating minerals as automatic fixes, these materials function as mindful anchors that complement the rhythm of your chosen technique. Below, we examine how two specific stones interact with meditation habits, drawing on geological properties, historical usage, and present-moment application.

  • Amethyst —

    Amethyst belongs to the quartz family, composed of silicon dioxide with trace iron and irradiation creating its violet hue. Historically, Greek writers associated the stone with mental clarity, while early Buddhist practitioners occasionally used it as a visual marker during sitting periods. When placed near the meditation mat, its cool surface and consistent weight provide a tactile reference point. Many people find it useful during Anapanasati breathing cycles, using the stone’s steady presence to return attention to the upper lip after wandering thoughts. The practice remains grounded in observable sensation rather than supernatural expectation.

    }
  • Clear Quartz —

    Clear Quartz forms from highly ordered silicon dioxide lattices, lacking the iron concentrations that tint other varieties. Ancient artisans often carved it into lenses or small weights, and some early meditation lineages referenced it as a tool for sharpening attention. Its transparent structure reflects light consistently, making it a practical companion for Visualization or Third-Eye sessions. Practitioners frequently rest the stone on a nearby table, glancing at its facets to reset focus when mental fog sets in. The routine relies on deliberate observation rather than automatic transformation.

    }

Eastern Traditions

南传：anapanasati/metta；藏传：Dzogchen 大圆满、Mahamudra 大手印；道家内丹 — 各传统有专属术语

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the main types of meditation?

How do I decide which style fits my schedule? Match the technique to your available time and mental state. Short sequences like Breath (Anapanasati) or Walking require only ten minutes and minimal setup, while Body Scan or Visualization benefit from longer, uninterrupted blocks. If you prefer structured phrases, Loving-Kindness (Metta) offers a clear framework. In the Theravada tradition, beginners often start with shamatha (止) concentration before introducing vipassana (观) insight work. The Tibetan tradition of Dzogchen (大圆满) and Mahamudra (大手印) emphasizes recognizing the nature of awareness through direct pointing-out instructions. Daoist inner alchemy describes specific timing windows such as 子午卯酉 活子时 for quiet sitting. Consistency matters more than duration when building attention habits. For bedtime wind-down routines, gentle Breath or Body Scan sequences invite calm without overstimulating the nervous system.
